docs.title: Welcome to the flyfield Documentation
docs.text: This documentation supports users and developers working with flyfield, a Python tool to automate white-box PDF form field extraction, filling, and data capture.

docs.title: Tutorials
docs.text:
• Worked Example Step-by-step walkthrough using a real-world tax form.
  • Quick Start Guide Fast-track for new users to start extracting and filling forms.

docs.title: How-To Guides
docs.text:
• Installation Guide Setup instructions and troubleshooting.
  • Usage and CLI Reference Detailed command-line options and workflows.
  • FAQ Frequently asked questions and common troubleshooting.

docs.title: Reference
docs.text:
• API Documentation Programmatic interface for developers.
  • Configuration and Constants Customization and advanced settings.

docs.title: Concepts and Explanations
docs.text:
• Introduction Overview and purpose of flyfield.
  • Process Explanation Detailed breakdown of internal workings.
  • Glossary Definitions of key terms used throughout the docs.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• The tool only supports vector PDFs with white boxes, not scanned rasterized PDFs.
  • Refer to tutorials and how-to guides first before diving into reference or concepts for a smoother learning curve.

Start your journey by exploring the Quick Start Guide or jump directly to the Worked Example.

docs.title: API Documentation for flyfield
docs.text: This documentation provides a detailed reference to the flyfield Python API for programmatically working with PDF forms that use white box placeholders.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The flyfield API helps automate workflows including:

  • Extracting white box placeholders in PDFs
  • Filtering and grouping detected fields
  • Generating interactive form fields programmatically
  • Filling form fields with data from CSV files
  • Extracting filled data back to CSV format

The API is designed to integrate with Python projects, offering programmable control beyond the CLI workflows.

docs.title: extract_boxes(pdf_path: str) -> List[Field]
docs.text:
• Typically called automatically during CLI workflows unless an input CSV file is supplied, avoiding manual extraction steps.
  • Scans the PDF at pdf_path for white box placeholders.
  • Returns a list of Field objects representing detected form field locations with metadata like page number, coordinates, and unique codes.

docs.title: filter_fields(fields: List[Field], min_size: Tuple[float,float]=None, max_size: Tuple[float,float]=None) -> List[Field]
docs.text:
• Filters out fields outside specified size bounds or other criteria.
  • Useful for refining detected candidates before form field generation.

docs.title: group_fields(fields: List[Field], max_dist: float=10.0) -> Dict[str, List[Field]]
docs.text:
• Groups fields spatially or by page for batch processing or logical association.
  • max_dist defines maximum distance between grouped fields.

docs.title: generate_fields(input_pdf: str, fields: List[Field], output_pdf: str) -> None
docs.text:
• Best practice is to run once per PDF version since this step can be time-consuming.
  • Creates interactive form fields in the PDF corresponding to white box locations.
  • Saves the resulting PDF as output_pdf.
  • Generated Python script for generation is storable for further edits.

docs.title: fill_fields(input_pdf: str, input_csv: str, output_pdf: str) -> None
docs.text:
• Fills the interactive form fields of input_pdf using data from CSV file input_csv.
  • Outputs a filled PDF as output_pdf.
  • CSV expected to contain code and fill columns matching field codes.

docs.title: capture_data(input_pdf: str, output_csv: str) -> None
docs.text:
• Extracts all filled form data from a completed PDF form and writes to CSV.
  • Includes field code and entered fill value.

docs.title: Field
docs.text:
Represents a detected form field placeholder.

Attribute Type Description code str Unique identifier for the field, e.g., 1-2-3 page int PDF page number bbox tuple Bounding box coordinates (x0, y0, x1, y1) value str Optional default or filled value

docs.title: Example Usage
docs.text:
from flyfield import extract_boxes, filter_fields, generate_fields, fill_fields, capture_data

# Extract white box placeholders
fields = extract_boxes("example.pdf")

# Filter fields by size if needed
fields = filter_fields(fields, min_size=(20, 10))

# Generate interactive fields PDF
generate_fields("example.pdf", fields, "example-fields.pdf")

# Fill fields with CSV data
fill_fields("example-fields.pdf", "data.csv", "example-fields-filled.pdf")

# Capture filled data back to CSV
capture_data("example-fields-filled.pdf", "captured_data.csv")

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• The API modules depend on libraries such as PyMuPDF and PyPDFForm for PDF manipulation.
  • Generated scripts during CLI usage correspond roughly to calls to API functions and can be used as templates.
  • Exception handling and logging is exposed via verbose and debug modes for troubleshooting.

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• Developer Guide for architectural details and contribution guidelines.
  • Worked Example for an end-to-end demonstration of the API in action.

docs.title: Configuration and Constants for flyfield
docs.text: This document describes configuration settings, constants, and customizable parameters available in flyfield to tailor processing and output.

docs.title: Configuration Overview
docs.text: flyfield uses a combination of CLI flags, environment variables, and internal constants to control behavior during extraction, field generation, filling, and capturing.

docs.title: CLI Configuration Options (Brief)
docs.text:
Common CLI options for controlling behavior include:

Option Description --input-pdf FILE Specifies the input PDF file. --pdf-pages PAGES Specifies page numbers or ranges to process. --input-csv FILE Specifies CSV file for input or captured data. --markup Generate a visual markup PDF for detected fields. --fields Generate interactive form fields in PDF. --fill Fill interactive fields with data from CSV. --capture Capture data from filled PDF back to CSV. --debug Enable detailed debug information and output.

docs.title: Internal Configuration Constants
docs.text:
Note: Detection only applies to pure white vector boxes matching size thresholds.

Parameter Description Default Value MIN_BOX_HEIGHT Minimum height (in points) for detected boxes 10 MIN_BOX_WIDTH Minimum width (in points) for detected boxes 20 MAX_BOX_HEIGHT Maximum height for detected boxes 80 MAX_BOX_WIDTH Maximum width for detected boxes 300 BOX_FILL_COLOR Required fill color (RGB) for detection Pure white (255,255,255) FIELD_CODE_PREFIX Prefix for autogenerated field codes Document-page-line-block naming scheme CSV_ENCODING Encoding expected for CSV input/output UTF-8

Extraction runs automatically unless an input CSV overrides the detection step.

docs.title: Customizing Field Detection
docs.text:
Customization can be done at the Python API level or by modifying the generated scripts:

  • Adjust filtering criteria such as box sizes or colors to tune detection sensitivity.
  • Override default field naming convention for unique or legacy integration requirements.
  • Modify CSV field formats or add custom metadata columns as needed.

docs.title: Environment Variables
docs.text:
flyfield respects the following environment variables to influence runtime:

Variable Description flyfield_LOG_LEVEL Sets logging verbosity (DEBUG, INFO, WARNING, ERROR) flyfield_CONFIG_FILE Path to an explicit YAML or JSON config file (future feature)

docs.title: Example: Adjust Minimum Box Size in Python Script
docs.text:
from flyfield import filter_fields

# Load detected fields list `fields`

# Filter out boxes smaller than 15x10 points

fields = filter_fields(fields, min_size=(15, 10))

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Most users can operate flyfield effectively using CLI flags without modifying constants.
  • Advanced users and developers can customize processing by editing Python scripts generated during CLI operations.
  • Future versions may add support for config files for easier persistent configuration.

For in-depth usage examples, see the User Guide and Worked Example.

docs.title: Developer Guide for flyfield
docs.text: This guide provides technical insight into the internal architecture of flyfield, instructions for setting up a development environment, and guidelines for contributing or customizing the project.

docs.title: Architecture Overview
docs.text:
flyfield is primarily implemented in Python and builds upon the following core components:

  • PDF Processing Layer: Uses libraries like PyMuPDF for PDF parsing and PyPDFForm for creating interactive form fields.

  • Extraction Module: Detects white box placeholders by analyzing vector graphics content in PDFs.

  • Filtering and Grouping Logic: Applies heuristics to filter and cluster detected white boxes to form meaningful fields.

  • Field Generation and Filling: Generates interactive fields and fills them programmatically based on CSV data.

  • Script Generation: Emits Python scripts for custom field creation and filling workflows, allowing advanced customization.

docs.title: Development Setup
docs.text:
• Always start testing workflows with a copied PDF to prevent overwriting originals.
  • Code repository contains source in flyfield/ directory; generated scripts and docs are separate.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Python 3.8 or higher
  • Git
  • Virtual environment tool (venv, virtualenv, or similar)
  • Required Python dependencies (see requirements.txt)

docs.title: Cloning and Installation
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/flywire/flyfield_docs.git
cd flyfield_docs
python -m venv env
source env/bin/activate   \# or .\env\Scripts\activate on Windows
pip install -r requirements.txt

docs.title: Running Tests
docs.text:
To run the automated test suite using pytest:

pytest

Tests cover core extraction, filtering, field generation, and filling logic.

docs.title: Code Structure
docs.text:
• flyfield/ — core source code
  • scripts/ — generated scripts for field creation and filling
  • tests/ — unit and integration tests
  • docs/ — documentation markdown files

docs.title: Customization Points
docs.text:
• Filtering Criteria: Adjust size, color, and position thresholds in extraction.py or filtering modules.
  • Field Naming: Modify logic generating field codes for compatibility with external systems.
  • Script Templates: Customize generated Python script templates in the templates/ folder.

docs.title: Contribution Guidelines
docs.text:
• Fork the repository and create a feature branch.
  • Follow PEP 8 coding style and add meaningful docstrings.
  • Add or update tests for any new features or bug fixes.
  • Submit a pull request with clear descriptions of changes.

docs.title: Debugging and Logging
docs.text: Use the --debug flags in the CLI or set the logging module level to debug when developing.

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• API Documentation for function-level details.
  • User Guide for CLI-focused instructions.
  • Worked Example for real case workflows.

Thank you for your interest in contributing to flyfield!

Happy coding and improving PDF form automation.

docs.title: Worked Example: Automating a Tax Return PDF Form
docs.text: This detailed step-by-step example demonstrates how to use flyfield for extracting, marking up, generating form fields, filling, and capturing data from a real complex form: the Australian Tax Office Trust Tax Return 2024. It starts with copying the original PDF to a new working filename to safeguard the source and uses this filename as the base for every output file in the workflow.

docs.title: Workflow Overview
docs.text:
Once the original PDF is copied, all subsequent steps work exclusively on the new working file. Each time flyfield is run without the --input-csv FILE option, it automatically extracts white box fields and generates the CSV, so a dedicated extraction step is not needed.

The five core automation steps are:

  1. Copy the original PDF to a working filename.
  2. Generate interactive form fields.
  3. Create a marked-up PDF to verify field positions.
  4. Fill form fields using CSV data.
  5. Capture filled form data back into a CSV for editing or reuse.

docs.title: Step 1: Copy the Source PDF
docs.text:
Begin by copying the original tax return PDF:

copy Trust-tax-return-2024.pdf example.pdf

Outputs:

  • example.pdf — copied working PDF file.

This protects the integrity of the original form by avoiding accidental overwrites or corruption. It also keeps all outputs organized and traceable by using the base filename example.pdf.

docs.title: Step 2: Generate Interactive PDF Form Fields
docs.text:
flyfield --input-pdf example.pdf --fields

Outputs:

  • example-fields.pdf — form with interactive fields.
  • example-field-generator.py — script used to generate fields.
  • example.csv — updated field codes and positions.

docs.title: Step 3: Create Markup PDF for Verification
docs.text:
flyfield --input-pdf example.pdf --markup

Outputs:

  • example-markup.pdf — visualizes all detected fields and codes for easy validation against the original.
  • example.csv — updated field codes and positions.

docs.title: Step 4: Fill Form Fields With CSV Data
docs.text:
flyfield --input-pdf example-fields.pdf --input-csv example-fields-filled-capture.csv --fill

Outputs:

  • example-fields-filled.pdf — form with CSV data filled in.
  • example-fields-filler.py — script used for filling.

docs.title: Step 5: Capture Data From Filled Form
docs.text:
flyfield --input-pdf example-fields-filled.pdf --capture

Outputs:

  • example-fields-filled-capture.csv — exported filled values for editing or reuse.

docs.title: Notes and Tips
docs.text:
• Starting with a copy safeguards the original form and simplifies file management.
  • Running flyfield generally triggers automatic extraction unless a CSV input file is provided.
  • Minimize repeated use of --fields on large PDFs because field generation can be slow.
  • Ensure CSV fields align perfectly before filling.
  • Use the markup step regularly to confirm all field detection and spot issues early.
  • The generated Python scripts enable advanced users to customize field generation and data filling.

docs.title: Frequently Asked Questions (FAQ)
docs.text: This FAQ section addresses common questions and troubleshooting tips for flyfield users.

docs.title: Q: I successfully installed flyfield using pipx but the command does not run. What should I do?
docs.text:
A: This often occurs because the directory where pipx installs executables is not in your system's PATH. To fix:

  1. Run the command: 
    pipx ensurepath
  2. Restart your terminal or command prompt to apply the PATH changes.
  3. Verify the installation by running: 
    flyfield --help
    Make sure you are running the command in the same user context where pipx is installed.

docs.title: Q: How do I check which version of flyfield I have?
docs.text:
A: Use the command:

flyfield --version

It will print the currently installed version.

docs.title: Q: How do I process only certain pages of a PDF?
docs.text: A: Use the --pdf-pages option with a comma-separated list of pages or ranges, like "1-3,5,7-10".

docs.title: Q: Do I need to run a separate extract step before generating fields?
docs.text: A: Extraction occurs automatically on input PDFs unless you supply an input CSV. Usually, a separate extract step is not needed.

docs.title: Q: How can I speed up processing when dealing with large PDFs?
docs.text: A: Running --fields is slow on large PDFs. Run it once per version and reuse generated CSVs and markup PDFs to speed up workflows.

docs.title: Q: The markup PDF shows fewer fields than expected. Why?
docs.text: A: Only pure white vector boxes within size thresholds are detected. Colored or shaded boxes, scanned PDFs, or boxes outside size limits will be missed. Use --debug to troubleshoot.

docs.title: Q: Why should I copy the source PDF before running commands?
docs.text: A: Working on a copy prevents overwriting or corrupting your original documents and helps keep outputs organized.

docs.title: Q: Can I customize field names or placement?
docs.text:
A: Yes. The Python scripts generated (*-field-generator.py and *-fields-filler.py) are editable. You can:

  • Change field naming schemes.
  • Adjust coordinates and sizes for fields.
  • Add special logic to merge or split fields.

Run the edited scripts manually with Python to regenerate PDFs as needed.

docs.title: Q: My filled PDF from --fill does not display data correctly in all viewers. What can I do?
docs.text:
A: Compatibility varies between PDF viewers. For best results:

  • Use Adobe Acrobat or well-supported PDF readers.
  • Avoid complex PDF overlays or security settings that might obscure fields.
  • Flatten PDF if needed using external tools after filling.

docs.title: Q: How do I extract data from a filled PDF form?
docs.text:
A: Use the --capture option:

flyfield --input-pdf filled-form.pdf --capture

This generates a CSV with all filled fields and their values for editing or further processing.

docs.title: Q: Does flyfield work on scanned PDFs?
docs.text: A: No. flyfield requires PDFs generated with vector graphics. Scanned images or raster PDFs are not supported.

docs.title: Q: Is there support for PDFs with text boxes instead of graphical white boxes?
docs.text:
A: flyfield focuses on detecting visible rectangular white boxes as placeholders, not interactive text boxes already embedded.

For further assistance, refer to the User Guide or Worked Example.

docs.title: Glossary of Terms for flyfield
docs.text: This glossary defines key terms and concepts used in the flyfield documentation and tooling.

docs.title: Key Terms
docs.text:
AcroForm A PDF form specification introduced in PDF 1.2 that enables interactive forms within PDF documents. Forms created with AcroForm contain elements like text fields, checkboxes, and buttons.

Field (Form Field) An interactive area in a PDF where users can enter or select data, such as text boxes, checkboxes, radio buttons, and drop-down menus.

Field Code A unique identifier automatically generated by flyfield for each detected white box in a PDF. Used to map form fields to CSV data.

Filled PDF A PDF form that has been completed with data, either manually by a user or programmatically by flyfield filling scripts.

Interactive Field A form field in a PDF that can be edited or filled using a PDF viewer or programmatically.

Markup PDF A generated PDF that visually annotates detected white box placeholders and field codes to help verify detection and placement accuracy.

Placeholder Box (White Box) A rectangular area in a vector PDF identified as a space where data should be entered. flyfield detects these based on color and size thresholds.

Working Copy PDF A duplicate of the original source PDF used as a working file for detection, field generation, filling, and capturing steps. Working copies prevent modification of the original and help organize outputs.

PyMuPDF A Python library used by flyfield for reading and manipulating PDF documents and their graphical elements.

PyPDFForm A Python library utilized to create interactive PDF form fields programmatically.

Raster PDF A PDF consisting primarily of scanned images or bitmap graphics rather than vector graphics, which flyfield cannot process effectively.

Vector PDF A PDF that uses vector graphics (lines, shapes) for rendering, required by flyfield for detecting white boxes accurately.

docs.title: Common Terms
docs.text:
BBox (Bounding Box) The rectangle defined by coordinates that enclose an element such as a form field or graphical object in a PDF.

CSV (Comma-Separated Values) A simple text format used to store tabular data. flyfield uses CSV files to import/export data for filling and capturing form fields.

Field Generator Script A Python script generated by flyfield that creates PDF form fields based on detected white boxes.

Field Filler Script A Python script generated to fill interactive PDF fields using data from CSV files.

docs.title: Accessibility Terms
docs.text:
Tab Order The sequence in which interactive fields receive focus when navigating a PDF form using keyboard input, important for usability.

Screen Reader Assistive technology that reads the content of a PDF aloud to users with visual impairments.

docs.title: Related Concepts
docs.text:
PDFBox An open-source Java library for working with PDF documents, forming much of the foundation underneath flyfield.

AcroForm vs. XFA Two types of PDF form technology. flyfield primarily works with AcroForm-based PDFs.

For further explanations and usage, see the relevant sections in the User Guide, API Documentation, and Quick Start Guide.

docs.title: Installation Guide for flyfield
docs.text: This guide explains how to install flyfield and get it ready for use on your system.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Python version 3.8 or higher
  • Access to a command-line terminal (Command Prompt, PowerShell, Terminal, etc.)

docs.title: 1. Recommended: Install via pipx
docs.text:
pipx allows installing Python applications in isolated environments, avoiding conflicts.

To install flyfield using pipx, run:

pipx install flyfield

After installation, the flyfield command will be available globally.

docs.title: 2. Alternative: Install via pip in a Virtual Environment
docs.text:
1. Create and activate a virtual environment (optional but recommended):

  2. On macOS/Linux: 

    python3 -m venv env
source env/bin/activate

  3. On Windows: 

    python -m venv env
.\env\Scripts\activate

  4. Install flyfield with pip:

pip install flyfield

  1. Verify installation:

  2. To verify the installed version of flyfield:

flyfield --version

docs.title: Development Installation (for contributors)
docs.text:
If you want to develop or contribute to the project:

  1. Clone the repository:
git clone https://github.com/flywire/flyfield_docs.git
cd flyfield_docs
  1. Install with development dependencies:
pip install -e .[dev]
  1. Run tests with pytest:
pytest

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text:
• If the flyfield command is not found after using pipx, run:
pipx ensurepath

Then restart your terminal.

  • If you encounter permission errors during installation, try adding --user to your pip install command:
pip install --user flyfield
  • Always keep your pip updated:
pip install --upgrade pip

docs.title: Introduction to flyfield
docs.text: Welcome to the flyfield documentation! This toolset helps automate the creation, filling, and data extraction of interactive PDF forms that feature white box placeholders.

docs.title: What is flyfield?
docs.text:
flyfield is a Python library and command-line interface (CLI) utility designed to:

  • Detect and extract white rectangular placeholders in vector PDFs.
  • Generate interactive form fields aligned precisely with those white boxes.
  • Fill PDF form fields programmatically using CSV data files.
  • Capture filled form data back into CSV for editing or reuse.

The project aims to simplify digitizing paper-like forms into machine-readable, automated workflows without manual data entry.

docs.title: Who is this for?
docs.text:
• Developers who need to automate PDF form workflows involving white box placeholders.
  • Workflow engineers who integrate PDF form interaction with spreadsheets or databases.
  • Technical teams digitizing complex tax, finance, or administrative forms.

docs.title: Why Use flyfield?
docs.text:
• Speed up form processing by automating field detection and filling.
  • Reduce human data entry errors through programmatic CSV integration.
  • Simplify workflows involving multiple PA forms or batch processing.
  • Open-source and extensible with modifiable Python scripts generated during CLI operations.

docs.title: Key Features
docs.text:
• Automatic Detection and Extraction of white box fields runs automatically unless input CSV data is provided.
  • Visual Markup PDFs for easy verification of detected fields.
  • Python Script Generation for advanced customization of form generation and filling.
  • CSV-Based Data Exchange to integrate with spreadsheets, databases, or downstream systems.

docs.title: How to Use This Documentation
docs.text:
• Start with the Quick Start Guide to set up and essential commands.
  • Explore the Worked Example for detailed real-world usage.
  • Dive into the User Guide for CLI commands and options.
  • Use the API Documentation for programmatic access beyond CLI.
  • Check FAQ for common questions and troubleshooting.
  • For internal architecture and contributions, see the Developer Guide.

We hope flyfield makes managing interactive PDF forms simpler and more efficient for your projects.

Happy automating!

docs.title: Processing Workflow of flyfield
docs.text: This document provides a detailed overview of the step-by-step processing workflow that flyfield follows to detect, generate, fill, and capture PDF form fields based on white box placeholders.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
flyfield automates the conversion of static white box PDF forms into interactive, fillable documents through a multi-stage process:

  1. Extraction of White Boxes
  2. Field Filtering and Grouping
  3. Interactive Field Generation
  4. Form Field Filling
  5. Data Capture from Filled Forms

docs.title: Step 1: Extraction of White Boxes
docs.text:
• Extraction runs automatically on input PDFs without an input CSV. You usually do not need a separate extraction step.
  • Vector graphics in the PDF are analyzed via PyMuPDF to find white rectangular placeholders.
  • Coordinates, pages, and unique field codes are recorded and output as a CSV.

docs.title: Step 2: Field Filtering and Grouping
docs.text:
• Raw extracted boxes may include noise; filtering removes boxes that do not fit expected dimensions or colors.
  • Grouping clusters related fields by proximity or page, helping organize form layout logically.
  • This stage is crucial to ensure fields correspond accurately to the intended form elements.

docs.title: Step 3: Interactive Field Generation
docs.text:
• Fields are generated only once per PDF version. This is computationally slower on large PDFs, so avoid repeated runs.
  • Interactive form fields are created based on the filtered field data (via PyPDFForm).
  • A marked-up PDF version is optionally produced to visualize field placement for verification.

docs.title: Step 4: Filling Form Fields
docs.text:
• PDF form fields can be programmatically filled by supplying a CSV file containing field codes and fill values.
  • Filling is done by running the generated fill scripts or via the CLI’s --fill option.
  • Output is a filled PDF form ready for use or review.

docs.title: Step 5: Data Capture from Filled Forms
docs.text:
• Filled PDF forms can be parsed to extract entered values back into CSV format.
  • This enables editing, auditing, or reusing the data in spreadsheets or databases.
  • The capture process reads interactive fields and maps their names and values into rows/columns.

docs.title: Additional Considerations
docs.text:
• The process requires vector PDFs with clear white box placeholders; scanned images or rasterized PDFs are unsupported.
  • Field extraction and generation steps may be time-consuming on large or complex PDFs; caching intermediate results is recommended.
  • Generated Python scripts provide hooks for customization at multiple stages of the workflow.

docs.title: Block Diagram
docs.text:
PDF Input → [White Box Extraction] → [Filtering/Grouping] → Field Generation → Markup → Filling → Data Capture

This workflow allows effective automation of PDF form processing, reducing manual effort and enabling digital document workflows.

For practical examples, see the Worked Example and related guides.

docs.title: Quick Start Guide for flyfield
docs.text: This quick start guide provides essential commands and workflow steps to begin using flyfield for PDF form automation.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Python 3.8 or above installed
  • flyfield package installed (recommended via pipx):
pipx install flyfield

After installation, verify your installed version by running:

flyfield --version

docs.title: Basic Workflow
docs.text: The key steps you'll typically perform using a copied PDF working file:

docs.title: 1. Copy the Source PDF (Recommended)
docs.text:
Begin by copying your original PDF to avoid overwriting:

copy your-form.pdf working-form.pdf

Outputs: - working-form.pdf — copied working PDF file.

This protects the integrity of the original form and organizes output files by using the working copy.

docs.title: 2. Generate Interactive PDF Form Fields
docs.text:
Extraction of white box fields runs automatically unless an input CSV is provided.

flyfield --input-pdf working-form.pdf --fields

Outputs: - working-form-fields.pdf — form with interactive fields. - working-form-field-generator.py — script used for generation. - working-form.csv — detected field codes and positions.

Tip: The generated script can be customized if needed before running.

docs.title: 3. Create Markup PDF for Verification
docs.text:
This step is optional but recommended to visually verify detected fields. The --pdf-pages option limits processing to specific pages:

flyfield --input-pdf working-form.pdf --pdf-pages "1,3-4" --markup

Outputs: - working-form-markup.pdf — PDF annotated with detected fields and codes.

Use this to confirm field detection accuracy and troubleshoot issues early.

docs.title: 4. Fill Form Fields Using CSV Data
docs.text:
Fill form fields with CSV data which must have code and fill columns:

flyfield --input-pdf working-form-fields.pdf --input-csv data.csv --fill

Outputs: - working-form-fields-filled.pdf — filled PDF form. - working-form-fields-filler.py — script used for filling.

docs.title: 5. Capture Data From Filled Form to CSV
docs.text:
Extract values entered or filled in the PDF form back to CSV for editing or reuse:

flyfield --input-pdf working-form-fields-filled.pdf --capture

Outputs: - working-form-fields-filled-capture.csv — CSV containing filled form data.

docs.title: Additional Tips
docs.text:
• Always work with a copied working PDF file to safeguard originals.
  • Avoid re-running --fields repeatedly on large PDFs; reuse generated CSV and markup outputs.
  • Use the --debug flag for detailed logs during troubleshooting.
  • Customize the generated Python scripts as needed for advanced workflows.
  • Keep backups of your PDFs and CSV files to prevent accidental overwrite.
  • Only vector PDFs with white boxes are supported; scanned PDFs are not compatible.

docs.title: Need More Help?
docs.text:
Start with the Worked Example for a practical guided workflow. See the full User Guide for detailed commands and options.

This quick start gives you the foundation to efficiently automate your white box PDF forms using flyfield.

docs.title: User Guide and CLI Reference for flyfield
docs.text: This guide covers all major command-line options, typical workflows, and tips for using flyfield effectively.

docs.title: Basic CLI Command Structure
docs.text: flyfield [options]

docs.title: Commonly Used Options
docs.text: Option Description --version Show the tool's version and exit --input-pdf FILE Specify the input PDF filename --pdf-pages PAGES Page numbers or ranges to process, e.g. "1-3,5,7" --input-csv FILE Specify CSV file for loading data or blocks --markup Generate a PDF with visual markup of fields --fields Generate and run script to add interactive fields --fill Generate and run script to fill fields from CSV --capture Extract filled form data back into a CSV --debug Output additional debug files -h, --help Show help message and exit

docs.title: Generate fields and extract data automatically
docs.text:
Extraction runs automatically if no CSV is provided.

flyfield --input-pdf form.pdf --fields
  • Produces form-fields.pdf, form-field-generator.py, and extracted form.csv.

docs.title: Add interactive fields to PDF
docs.text:
flyfield --input-pdf form.pdf --fields
  • Warning: This can be slow for large PDFs. Run once per PDF version to cache.

docs.title: Create markup PDF to verify field locations
docs.text:
flyfield --input-pdf form.pdf --markup
  • Generates form-markup.pdf for visual verification.

docs.title: Fill PDF form using CSV data
docs.text:
flyfield --input-pdf form-fields.pdf --input-csv data.csv --fill
  • Fills fields based on CSV code,fill mappings.

docs.title: Capture filled data back to CSV
docs.text:
flyfield --input-pdf form-fields-filled.pdf --capture
  • Saves filled data to CSV for reuse or editing.

docs.title: Options Combination
docs.text:
• You can combine options in a single command. For example:
flyfield --input-pdf form.pdf --markup --fields
  • Note the --fields process can be slow on large PDFs; consider caching outputs.

docs.title: Editing Generated Scripts
docs.text:
• The *-field-generator.py and *-fields-filler.py scripts generated in field and fill steps can be edited to customize field placement, naming, or filling logic.
  • After editing, run the scripts manually with Python to generate the desired PDF output.

docs.title: Tips and Troubleshooting
docs.text:
• Work with copied PDFs to safeguard originals.
  • Avoid rerunning --fields repeatedly; prefer reuse of CSV and markup outputs.
  • Use --debug for detailed logs.
  • The tool supports PDFs with vector graphics and strict white box fields; scanned PDFs are not supported.
  • Ensure CSV files are UTF-8 encoded and follow the code, fill format.

This guide should help users leverage the full power of flyfield for automated PDF form processing.

For additional details, refer to the Worked Example and Quick Start Guide.
